data.title: PepsiCo close to settlement with activist investor Elliott - WSJ
data.description:
Prominent activist investor Elliott Investment Management is nearing a settlement agreement with PepsiCo (PEP) after taking a large stake in the beverage and snacks behemoth in September.

Details of the settlement weren't known, though an announcement could happen soon, according to a WSJ report on Thursday, which cited people familiar with the matter.

A potential settlement comes after Elliott  took a $4 billion stake in PepsiCo (PEP) in September and pushed for engagement with the company's board. Elliott wants PepsiCo to evaluate refranchising its bottling network, and divesting non-core and underperforming assets.

PepsiCo (PEP) in October announced that its chief financial officer was retiring and being replaced by former Walmart U.S. CFO Steve Schmitt.

data.title: CROs fall as FDA head Makary says drug approvals will require fewer trials
data.description:
• Contract research organizations such as Charles River Laboratories (CRL), ICON (ICLR), and IQVIA (IQV), are trading in the red on Thursday after FDA Commissioner Marty Makary said that pharmas will only need to submit results from one pivotal trial for most new drug approval applications.
  • "You can achieve the same statistical power with one trial as you would with two trials when it's designed and controlled appropriately," he told STAT. 
  • Medpace (MEDP) is the hardest hit CRO so far , down ~4%, followed by IQVIA and Fortrea (FTRE), both off ~2%.

data.title: Knife River rated Neutral in new coverage by J.P. Morgan; 'show me' margin story
data.description:
J.P. Morgan analyst Adrian Huerta on Thursday initiated coverage of Knife River (KNF) with a Neutral rating. The firm describes Knife River (KNF) as a vertically integrated aggregates and construction materials supplier with a distinctive presence across 14 Western and Central U.S. states, markets where it often holds the No. 1 share and faces limited direct competition from national players.

Knife River’s (KNF) heavy exposure to public infrastructure work (80% to 87% of its contracting backlog and about half of materials revenue tied to government-funded projects) provides above-average revenue visibility, Huerta wrote. Management’s emphasis on dynamic pricing, operational programs such as EDGE and PIT Crews, and a strategy of aggregates-led acquisitions could support long-term improvement in ebitda margins, which the company ultimately aims to lift above 20%.

Still, J.P. Morgan sees limited visibility in the near and medium term. Huerta points to lower free cash flow conversion versus peers, the absence of plans to return capital to shareholders, and the need to prove out early-stage margin initiatives as reasons to remain on the sidelines, even as the balance of risks trends positive if execution improves.

The firm notes that Knife River’s (KNF) geographic footprint, situated in higher-growth regions but still largely mid-tier markets, may constrain how far its pricing power can expand. Dynamic pricing could help close the roughly 20% price gap between the company and larger national aggregates competitors, but structural limits in these markets may temper the gains.

Knife River’s (KNF) vertical integration allows it to capture more ebitda dollars by controlling materials, contracting, and downstream project execution. But its free cash flow conversion rate of 25% to 30% trails national peers, which are closer to 50%. According to Huerta, achieving a 20% ebitda margin, compared with J.P. Morgan’s forecast of 15.6% for 2025, would lift FCF conversion toward 45%, and reducing maintenance capex to 5% of revenue (from roughly 6% today) could add another five percentage points.

J.P. Morgan set a December 2026 price target of $83, implying roughly 11% upside and valuing the company at 9.4 times forward enterprise value to ebitda, a modest discount to peer Eagle Materials (EXP).

data.title: Spot crypto can now trade on CFTC-registered exchanges
data.description:
U.S.-listed spot crypto products will start trading for the first time on registered futures exchanges, the Commodity Futures Trading Commission said on Thursday, a move that signals digital assets are being pulled deeper into traditional markets.

“Recent events on offshore exchanges have shown us how essential it is for Americans to have more choice and access to safe, regulated U.S. markets," CFTC Acting Chairman Caroline Pham said in a statement.

The move advances the Trump administration's push to make the U.S. a global hub for crypto, tracking recommendations from the President's Working Group on Digital Assets, which earlier in the year outlined a crypto agenda for regulators.

In September, the CFTC launched an initiative inviting interest stakeholders to submit feedback on the use of tokenized collateral, including stablecoins, in derivatives markets.

data.title: Semiconductor billings surge in October with DRAM up 90%: Wells Fargo
data.description:
Semiconductor billings surged 33% year over year to total $71.3B in October, according to Wells Fargo, citing the most recent data from the Semiconductor Industry Association.

Dynamic random access memory, or DRAM, accounted for much of the jump as DRAM billings rocketed 90% year over year to reach $12.82B. The most prolific producers of DRAM include Micron Technology (MU), Samsung (OTCPK:SSNLF) and SK Hynix. NAND billings increased 13% year over year to total $5.13B.

"We would note that SIA's implied NAND capacity ship estimates were materially revised for September—thus implying declines in $/GB (i.e., -16% m/m in Oct?), which we question given the current market dynamics," said Wells Fargo analysts, led by Aaron Rakers, in an industry note.

Microprocessor billings totaled $5.98B in October, which was a year-over-year increase of 16%. However, despite the jump in billings, the total number of MPUs shipped declined 4% year over year.

Analog billings were even stronger, increasing by 18% year over year to total $7.93B. Units shipped climbed 11% year over year.

Microcontroller billings also increased 18% year over year to $1.88B, with shipments up 21%. MOSFET power billings gained 19% year over year to $1.02B.

Semiconductor stocks were demonstrating mixed action during Thursday trading. Micron was down 3%, while Nvidia (NVDA) had ticked up 2%. Intel (INTC) had dropped 6%, AMD (AMD) had inched down 0.5% and Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing (TSM) had declined 1%. Texas Instruments (TXN) had slipped 1% and Analog Devices (ADI) was up 0.2%.

data.title: U.S. stocks see modest gains after trade struggle due to rate cuts anticipation
data.description:
Wall Street's major averages shyly held onto gains on Thursday as investors assessed Meta Platforms' (META) rise after Mark Zuckerberg is reportedly cutting Metaverse spending, and jobless claims came lower than expected.

The benchmark S&P 500 (SP500) was last +0.1% in afternoon trade, while the Nasdaq Composite (COMP:IND) was +0.2%, and the Dow (DJI) was +0.1%.

Meta Platforms (META) -- last +4% -- rose as Bloomberg reported that Mark Zuckerberg said that the company is looking at cutting Metaverse efforts by up to 30%.

On the economic side, the initial jobless claims retreated to below 200K and were better than expected. U.S.-based employers announced 71.32K job cuts in November, down 53% from the 153.07K announced in October.

“Initial claims collapsed last week to a three-year low. The Bureau of Labor Statistics, which compiles this report, did not note any special factors. So, as I highlighted in red all year, the less-than-5-day workweeks distort this series,” said Jim Bianco, president and macro strategist at Bianco Research.

“Last week was Thanksgiving. Previously, these short workweeks resulted in a spike in claims, but Thanksgiving week saw a collapse. Even if you adjust for this week for Thanksgiving, the data still doesn't suggest the Fed should cut rates next week,” he added.

The Federal Reserve will announce its interest rate decision next week. Odds for a 25-basis-point cut are at 87%, according to the CME FedWatch Tool.

In other news, President Donald Trump's aides were reportedly in talks about appointing Treasury Secretary Scott Bessent as the top White House economic adviser, in addition to his current role, if Kevin Hassett is picked as the next Federal Reserve chair.

Over in the bond market, the 10-year Treasury yield (US10Y) rose 3 basis points to 4.10%, while the 2-year yield (US2Y) rose 4 basis points to 3.54%.

data.title: VinFast plans to expand capacity in India for electric bus and electric scooter production
data.description:
VinFast Auto Ltd. (VFS) announced on Thursday that it struck a deal with the government of Tamil Nadu in India to allocate approximately 200 hectares of land in the SIPCOT Industrial Park in Thoothukudi to support the development of the company’s existing facility. The electric vehicle maker said the milestone provides an opportunity for it to broaden its product portfolio from electric cars to electric buses, e-scooters, and charging infrastructure.

Under the terms of the memorandum of understanding, the government of Tamil Nadu will allocate approximately 500 acres of land adjacent to VinFast’s (VFS) existing facility in Thoothukudi. The state will also provide support in securing required permits and in establishing essential infrastructure connections such as electricity, water, internal road access, drainage, and waste management.

As part of a second phase of its existing $2 billion commitment, VinFast (VFS) will invest $500 million in Thoothukudi to develop new dedicated workshops and production lines for electric buses and e-scooters, covering manufacturing, assembly, testing, and other related operations.

The existing Thoothukudi facility covers 400 acres (160 hectares) and is equipped with internationally standardized production lines. It has an initial annual capacity of 50,000 electric vehicles and is being expanded to 150,000 units. 

"The proposed expansion of the Tamil Nadu plant will enable us to broaden our product lineup in India, from electric cars to electric buses and e-scooters, allowing us to meet a wider range of customer needs," highlighted Vingroup Asia CEO Pham Sanh Chau.

Shares of VinFast (VFS) are up more than 4% this week but still show double-digit year-to-date decline.

data.title: Top retail stocks by YTD performance as Costco flops
data.description:
In the face of consumer spending concerns, government shutdowns, and reduced SNAP payments, certain retail stocks have demonstrated remarkable resilience.

We present the best performing retail stocks as Costco (COST) fails to deliver gains year-to-date, even after another consistent month of comparable sales growth in November. The company has also recently been on the spotlight after suing the U.S. government to receive a full refund of tariffs it already paid amid the expected Supreme Court rule on whether President Donald Trump's levies are illegal.

The big-box warehouse club retail store is down 8.5% over the last 52 weeks and down 2.5% year-to-date, despite its solid November sales figures of $23.64 billion (up 8.1% year-over-year) with comparable sales rising 6.9%.

This stock screen highlights the retail sector’s top performers, showcasing companies across various segments including apparel, automotive, and broadline retail.

Each stock is presented with its corresponding Seeking Alpha Quant Rating where available, providing additional context beyond pure performance metrics. This comprehensive view helps identify not just which stocks have performed well historically, but which might be positioned for continued strength according to analyst evaluations.

Here are the top U.S. retail companies, ranked by year-to-date performance:

data.title: Donaldson raises EPS guidance to $4.03 amid record sales and margin expansion
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Donaldson Company, Inc. (DCI) Q1 2026

Management View

  • CEO Tod Carpenter opened by highlighting strong first quarter results with record sales of $935 million, an all-time high operating profit margin of 15.5%, and record earnings per share of $0.94, representing a 13% increase over the prior year. Carpenter emphasized growth across Mobile Aftermarket, Power Generation, Food and Beverage, and Disk Drive, stating, "We delivered once again on our commitments to all of our stakeholders, our customers, our shareholders and our employees."
  • Carpenter discussed expanded partnerships, notably with NAPA, and continued share gains in the independent channel, with nearly double-digit sales growth. He noted construction market strength offsetting agricultural softness in Off-Road, and highlighted full order books in Power Generation due to data center and AI infrastructure demand.
  • In Life Sciences, Carpenter pointed to over 20% growth in Food and Beverage and Disk Drive segments, with share gains and investments in new disk drive technologies such as HAMR.
  • Carpenter reported the estimated annual tariff impact has been reduced to $25 million from $35 million, attributing this to pricing actions and supply chain optimization. He also mentioned ongoing cost optimization and footprint initiatives to drive structural efficiencies, expecting completion by the second half of fiscal year.
  • CFO Brad Pogalz stated, "Operating margin was a first quarter record of 15.5%, up 60 basis points over prior year with an incremental margin over 30%. Adjusted EPS was $0.94, up 13% and cash conversion was strong at 101% due to improved working capital management."

Outlook

  • The company raised its full year operating margin guidance by 10 basis points to a range of 16.2% to 16.8%, with the midpoint at 16.5% and an implied incremental margin above 40%.
  • Donaldson increased its fiscal 2026 EPS guidance by $0.03 to a range of $3.95 to $4.11, or $4.03 at the midpoint.
  • The sales growth outlook remains between 1% and 5%, with record sales guidance at $3.8 billion. Sales growth is expected in each segment, and cash conversion is projected between 85% and 95%.
  • No change to sales guidance except for On-Road within Mobile Solutions, which is now expected to be flat versus prior year, revised from previous high single-digit growth due to project timing.
  • Management expects Life Sciences profit margin to be mid-single digits for the full year and forecast profitability in every quarter, noting first quarter benefited from project timing.

Financial Results

  • First quarter sales reached $935 million, a 4% year-over-year increase. Mobile Solutions sales were $598 million, up 5%; Aftermarket sales were $480 million, up 7%. Off-Road sales of $95 million increased 6%. On-Road sales declined 27% to $23 million.
  • Industrial Solutions sales were $258 million, flat year-over-year. Industrial Filtration Solutions sales grew 2% to $216 million; Aerospace and Defense sales were $42 million, down 7%.
  • Life Sciences sales reached $79 million, up 13%.
  • Operating expense as a rate of sales improved to 19.9% from 20.7% a year ago. Gross margin was 35.4%, down 20 basis points from prior year. Cash conversion was 101%.
  • Segment pretax profit margin for Mobile Solutions was 18.6%. Industrial Solutions pretax margin was 12.5%, down from 15.9% in the prior year. Life Sciences pretax margin rose to 9.2% from a loss of 7.6%.

Q&A

  • Bryan Blair, Oppenheimer: Asked about IFS revenue by type and connected machines growth. CFO Pogalz replied both first-fit and replacement were up, especially in power generation, and CEO Carpenter confirmed the company is on target to connect between 2,000 and 3,000 dust collectors this year.
  • Blair queried about realized structural benefits from footprint optimization. Pogalz explained, "In terms of structural efficiencies to date, very limited...the next tranche of work is going to be...start-up in the new home of this production."
  • Angel Castillo, Morgan Stanley: Asked about pricing trends and regional performance. COO Lewis described pricing as "fairly normalized" and competitive, and Carpenter described Europe as the "most consistent region," cautioning on China despite recent sales gains.
  • Adam Farley, Stifel: Asked about mobile aftermarket share gains and OE channel restocking. Carpenter responded growth is from both new share gains and prior wins, with typical OE restocking behavior factored into guidance.
  • Farley also asked about industrial decrementals; Pogalz cited pressure from higher operating costs, while Lewis added that most footprint optimization work is in that space with benefits expected later in the year.
  • Brian Drab, William Blair: Probed industrial outlook acceleration; Pogalz pointed to project timing and supplier issues, and anticipated sales and profitability to build through the year.
  • Drab asked about Disk Drive business drivers; Lewis attributed strength to AI and cloud storage demand and technological change, while Pogalz noted it represents a few percent of total sales.
  • Drab further asked about data center opportunities; Lewis described multiple business touchpoints with data centers, especially in power generation and microelectronics.
  • Laurence Alexander, Jefferies: Sought detail on share gains and power generation growth. Carpenter said mature markets see low single-digit share gains, and power generation gains are increasing, with full order books for the year.
  • Alexander asked about future capacity needs; Carpenter said current investments position the company well for future demand without the need for "typical rushes."
  • Timothy Thein, Raymond James: Asked about aftermarket growth expectations; Carpenter confirmed growth is expected to moderate after a strong first quarter, especially for OE channels.
  • Thein asked about incremental margin benefits from footprint optimization; Pogalz highlighted that most of the profit is expected in the second half, driven by gross margin expansion as projects complete.
  • Robert Mason, Baird: Questioned reasons for early guidance increase. Carpenter cited portfolio diversification and higher highs in growth areas, emphasizing transparency and confidence in execution.
  • Mason inquired about power generation growth given full capacity; Carpenter said mid-single-digit growth is expected, with potential upside if execution exceeds expectations.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts were generally positive but pressed for greater detail on regional trends, realized cost savings, share gains, and the sustainability of margin improvements, while seeking reassurance about guidance credibility.
  • Management maintained a confident and constructive tone throughout, emphasizing execution, transparency, and structural advantages, with Carpenter stating, "We feel very good and confident about where we are to execute the year."
  • Compared to the previous quarter, both analysts and management displayed slightly increased confidence, with less focus on macro headwinds and more on execution and realized benefits.
  • Management's tone in prepared remarks was confident and forward-looking, while in Q&A, it was transparent and responsive, acknowledging some uncertainties but highlighting a strong outlook.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • Guidance for operating margin and EPS was raised compared to the previous quarter, with operating margin midpoint up 10 basis points and EPS midpoint up $0.03.
  • The sales outlook for On-Road within Mobile Solutions was revised downward to flat from high single-digit growth, attributed to project timing.
  • Analysts’ focus shifted from concerns about end market troughs and supply chain challenges in the prior quarter to details on execution, share gains, and margin expansion in the current quarter.
  • Management's confidence increased, particularly in addressing tariff impacts, cost optimization, and backlogs. Analyst sentiment moved from cautious optimism to a more positive, fact-seeking tone.
  • Key metrics such as Mobile Solutions and Life Sciences showed stronger year-over-year improvement, while Industrial Solutions margins declined due to mix and cost headwinds.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management noted continued cyclical headwinds in agriculture and On-Road, and highlighted the complexity of ramping up power generation at full capacity.
  • The company is still completing major footprint optimization projects, with limited realized efficiencies to date and most benefits expected in the second half.
  • Tariff impacts remain a risk, though mitigated through pricing and supply chain actions, with the annualized impact now estimated at $25 million.
  • Analysts raised concerns about the sustainability of pricing, regional unevenness (especially in Latin America and China), and the lumpiness of project-driven businesses.

Final Takeaway

Donaldson Company delivered record-setting first quarter results with broad-based growth and ongoing margin expansion, prompting an increase in both operating margin and EPS guidance for fiscal 2026. The company is benefiting from strong execution in key segments, effective cost optimization, and disciplined capital allocation. While some end markets remain challenged and structural efficiencies from ongoing projects are yet to be fully realized, management remains confident in the outlook, supported by full order books, continued market share gains, and an improving tariff environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: Kroger sees continued pressure on consumer but e-commerce to be profitable in 2026
data.description:
Weighed down by costs tied to the sale of its specialty pharmacy business and higher employee contributions to multi-employer pension plans, Kroger (KR) swung to an unadjusted loss in the third quarter, while sales were below Wall Street’s expectations. Coupled with a lackluster outlook for the full year, the stock retreated to its lowest level in almost a year, suffering its largest one-day drop in more than three years.

Noting a bifurcation in spending by income levels, Kroger (KR) saw increased spending by higher income consumers, but lower income customers remained pressured and more deliberate in spending on non-food items. 

“Inflation and pause in SNAP benefits added incremental pressure to third quarter identical sales less fuel…and we’re seeing no meaningful improvement in the consumer environment in the fourth quarter,” Kroger management said during the company’s earnings call. This drove a revision to FY25 identical-store sales growth ex-fuel guidance to up by 2.8% to 3.0% from earlier guidance of +2.7% to +3.4%.

“We saw increased caution and uncertainty among consumers, particularly in October and November…and consumers are becoming more selective,” interim CEO Ron Sargent said on the call, noting the increased competition with the grocery space as consumers look for greater value.

For the current quarter, Kroger (KR) also anticipates a 30-40 basis point headwind to identical sales ex-fuel due to the impact of the Inflation Reduction Act on the pharmacy business. Beginning January 1 Kroger (KR) will record drug sales at the lower prices, while manufacturers will fully reimburse the company for the difference in cost through rebates. These will subsequently be recorded as an offset to COGS.

Fortunately, e-commerce is expected to be profitable in 2026.

Management’s measured comments regarding the consumer environment with the company’s efforts to right-size its e-commerce business helped mitigate the pressure on shares during the call and contributed to recovery off the intra-day low of $60.96.
